title: Kth Smallest in a BST
code: public int KthSmallest(TreeNode root, int k)
while (curr is not null)        // dive left
if (--k == 0) return curr.val;  // k-th visited in sorted order
return -1; // unreachable for valid k in [1, n]

frames.caption: Goal: find the 3rd smallest value. Start curr at the root (5) with an empty stack; we will run an iterative inorder and stop after the k-th visit.
frames.note: Inorder traversal of a BST emits values in sorted order, so the k-th value it produces is the k-th smallest. Doing it iteratively with an explicit stack lets us STOP early after the k-th visit, touching only O(h + k) nodes instead of all n — far cheaper than collecting the whole sorted list.

frames.caption: Dive left: push 5 onto the stack, then move curr to its left child (3).
frames.note: The "dive left" loop walks to the smallest unvisited node, pushing every node along the way onto the stack. The stack remembers the ancestors we still owe a visit, so we can return to each of them after its left subtree is fully drained. When we can dive no further left, the top of the stack is the next node in sorted order.

frames.caption: Dive left: push 3 onto the stack, then move curr to its left child (1).
frames.note: The "dive left" loop walks to the smallest unvisited node, pushing every node along the way onto the stack. The stack remembers the ancestors we still owe a visit, so we can return to each of them after its left subtree is fully drained. When we can dive no further left, the top of the stack is the next node in sorted order.

frames.caption: Dive left: push 1 onto the stack, then move curr to its left child (null — can dive no further).
frames.note: The "dive left" loop walks to the smallest unvisited node, pushing every node along the way onto the stack. The stack remembers the ancestors we still owe a visit, so we can return to each of them after its left subtree is fully drained. When we can dive no further left, the top of the stack is the next node in sorted order.

frames.caption: Stack non-empty: pop 1. This is inorder visit #1 → the 1st smallest so far.
frames.note: Popping the stack visits a node — and because we always exhaust the left subtree before pushing/visiting a node, pops come out in inorder, i.e. SORTED order on a BST. Each pop is the next-smallest value, so the k-th pop is exactly the k-th smallest. After visiting, we step right to let that subtree contribute its (larger) values.

frames.caption: --k = 2 (not 0 yet), so keep going. We have not reached the k-th smallest.
frames.note: Decrementing k on every visit turns "k-th smallest" into a simple countdown: the visit where --k reaches 0 is the answer, and we return immediately. This EARLY STOP is the whole point of the iterative form — we touch only O(h + k) nodes instead of all n, never building the full sorted list.

frames.caption: Move curr to 1's right child (null); the outer loop will dive left into it for the next-larger values.
frames.note: After visiting a node, its left subtree and the node itself are done; only its right subtree can hold larger, not-yet-visited values. We set curr to node.right and let the outer loop dive left into it — that resumes the inorder walk at the next-larger region of the tree.

frames.caption: Stack non-empty: pop 3. This is inorder visit #2 → the 2nd smallest so far.
frames.note: Popping the stack visits a node — and because we always exhaust the left subtree before pushing/visiting a node, pops come out in inorder, i.e. SORTED order on a BST. Each pop is the next-smallest value, so the k-th pop is exactly the k-th smallest. After visiting, we step right to let that subtree contribute its (larger) values.

frames.caption: --k = 1 (not 0 yet), so keep going. We have not reached the k-th smallest.
frames.note: Decrementing k on every visit turns "k-th smallest" into a simple countdown: the visit where --k reaches 0 is the answer, and we return immediately. This EARLY STOP is the whole point of the iterative form — we touch only O(h + k) nodes instead of all n, never building the full sorted list.

frames.caption: Move curr to 3's right child (4); the outer loop will dive left into it for the next-larger values.
frames.note: After visiting a node, its left subtree and the node itself are done; only its right subtree can hold larger, not-yet-visited values. We set curr to node.right and let the outer loop dive left into it — that resumes the inorder walk at the next-larger region of the tree.

frames.caption: Dive left: push 4 onto the stack, then move curr to its left child (null — can dive no further).
frames.note: The "dive left" loop walks to the smallest unvisited node, pushing every node along the way onto the stack. The stack remembers the ancestors we still owe a visit, so we can return to each of them after its left subtree is fully drained. When we can dive no further left, the top of the stack is the next node in sorted order.

frames.caption: Stack non-empty: pop 4. This is inorder visit #3 → the 3rd smallest so far.
frames.note: Popping the stack visits a node — and because we always exhaust the left subtree before pushing/visiting a node, pops come out in inorder, i.e. SORTED order on a BST. Each pop is the next-smallest value, so the k-th pop is exactly the k-th smallest. After visiting, we step right to let that subtree contribute its (larger) values.

frames.caption: --k is now 0 → 4 is the 3rd smallest. Return 4 immediately (early stop — node 5, 8, 9 are never visited).
frames.note: Decrementing k on every visit turns "k-th smallest" into a simple countdown: the visit where --k reaches 0 is the answer, and we return immediately. This EARLY STOP is the whole point of the iterative form — we touch only O(h + k) nodes instead of all n, never building the full sorted list.
